query: slipped capital epiphysis incidence rate in adolescents aged 10-16

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: SCFE is one of the most common hip pathologies affecting pre-adolescent and adolescent patients, occurring in approximately 10.8/100,000 (Range: 0.33/100,000 to 24.8/100,000). The single most significant risk factor for SCFE is obesity. [4] Other risk factors include male sex, periods of rapid growth, prior radiation therapy to the hip, and retroversion of the acetabulum or femoral head, both of which increase mechanical shear forces across the physis. The average age of onset is 11.2 years in females and 12.0 years in males. The left hip is more commonly affected, but approximately 25% (Range: 8 to 50%) of cases are bilateral. [2] Recent literature has shown increased incidence at a younger age and more bilateral cases being reported as a possible reflection of increased rates of childhood obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The Loder classification is essential in prognosis. The original study in 1993 found that a patient with SCFE classified as stable did not develop osteonecrosis, whereas 47% of the patients with SCFE who were considered unstable went on to develop osteonecrosis. More recent studies, including a systematic review, have confirmed these findings. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Defect(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Seitsalo et al. found that 23% of their 272 children/adolescents demonstrated slip progression greater than 10% at approximately 16 years follow up. [25] Another study, Danielson et al., found 3% of their 311 patients demonstrated slip progression greater than 20% at approximately four years follow up. [26] Patients are likely most vulnerable to slip progression during growth spurts. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The clinician should consider laboratory testing to evaluate for possible underlying medical conditions such as endocrine or renal disorders if the patient is less than ten years of age or pre-pubertal, has short stature (more than two standard deviations below mean for age), or weight below the 50th percentile for age. Initial laboratory testing can include thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level, thyroxine level (free T4), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and serum creatinine. [3] Further testing can be performed in consultation with appropriate pediatric specialists, such as pediatric endocrinology.

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most cases of SCFE are idiopathic. Often, there is no history of trauma or injury before the onset of symptoms. Additionally, an association has been reported between the development of SCFE and patients with endocrine disorders (including hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, panhypopituitarism, and growth hormone deficiency), renal disorders, and Down syndrome. [3] Hypothyroidism is the most common etiology of nonidiopathic SCF. Hence, SCFE presentation in a child younger than ten years old or weighing less than the 50th percentile is an indication of endocrine workup.

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The Southwick Slip Angle Classification grades the severity of SCFE based on the difference in the epiphyseal diaphyseal angle between the affected hip and the unaffected/contralateral hip, both in anteroposterior and lateral radiographs of the hip. Mild cases < 30 degrees, moderate cases 30-50 degrees, and severe cases with more than 50 degrees of difference. In bilateral cases, a set reference is used for comparison: 145 degrees on the anteroposterior radiographs and 10 degrees on the lateral radiographs.

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Temporal Classification is based on the duration of symptoms. It is seldom used as it has no bearing on prognosis. SCFE is considered acute if symptoms are present for less than three weeks, chronic if symptoms have persisted for more than three weeks, or acute on chronic if a patient has worsening symptoms for more than three weeks. SCFE can also be graded based on the percentage of slippage. Grade I up to 33%, Grade II 34 to 50 %, and Grade III over 50%.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

Indirect‑site interventions linked to the femur include **replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate**, **revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components**, and **suture of periosteum of femur**. Preventive care is represented by **prophylactic treatment of femur**.
